seed: safety vs exposure; pressure_vs_release; split_vertical; echo_right; braid against ripple; pressure_border; thin_vs_heavy; vessel; softness_severity | abstract variation request 279; target batch count 500
title: Exposure and Shelter
reasoning: This composition represents the emotional tension between safety and exposure, using a palette that speaks to vulnerability and protection. The warm brown and shelter green suggest stability and safety, while the pale skin and open blue evoke feelings of vulnerability and openness. The collision occurs in the central band, where the color warm brown meets open blue, creating visual tension and echoing the themes of pressure versus release. The use of broken pixels introduces intentional imperfections, symbolizing the fraught nature of safety and exposure—neither state is entirely secure or completely free. The pattern of stacked bands reflects layers of emotion, shifting from stable, protective colors at the top to lighter, more vulnerable tones below, resonating with the overarching theme of exposure versus shelter.

layout.name: stacked_bands
layout.description: Rows behave like emotional strata, shifting density and temperature from band to band.

composition.name: exposure_vs_shelter
composition.idea: A pale exposed center is either protected by a frame or left open to incoming marks.

referenceExample.title: Dread Releasing
referenceExample.pattern: heavy top corner loosens into pale open space

generationRules: Pick 1 pattern type for emotion A.
Pick 1 pattern type for emotion B.
Pick 1 shape seed as the central object.
Apply 1 to 6 broken pixels to create imperfection.
Leave at least 10 percent negative space unless the mood is panic, rage, urgency, euphoria, or pressure.
Use the shape seed as an abstract organizing metaphor, not as a literal icon.
Make the two selected pattern types visibly collide, overlap, interrupt, or trade space.
Prefer 4 to 8 distinct hex colors, with at least one quiet field color and one accent color.
Keep the 8x8 grid readable at thumbnail size by preserving clear large-scale structure.
Let one emotion dominate 55 to 70 percent of the square unless the selected layout demands balance.
Use broken pixels as intentional disruptions, scars, repairs, leaks, or false echoes.
Avoid perfectly even distribution unless the chosen composition is control, serenity, clarity, or certainty.
Give the main collision a location: center, edge, corner, diagonal, border, or channel.
generationSpec.negativeSpaceRule: Leave at least 10 percent negative space unless the chosen emotional tone needs dense pressure.
